title: Lace texture pressing into clay slabs
subtitle: Transferring fabric lace patterns into soft clay surfaces
excerpt: A technique for pressing fabric lace, mesh, or lace ribbon into a soft clay slab to transfer its pattern as a surface texture. Works on any clay body at any scale. The impressed texture can then be used as decorative surface on tiles, dishes, or wall panels.
sourceNotes: Textile impression into clay is found across many ceramic traditions. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness.
Unfired clay in any drying stage. For air-dry clay, any stage before full drying.

content.text: Pressing a piece of lace into a soft clay
slab

transfers the lace pattern as a repeating relief texture. The technique works with any open-weave fabric: cotton lace, synthetic mesh, loose burlap, eyelet fabric, even the net bags fruit comes in. The texture depth depends on how firmly the fabric is pressed and the weave size of the material.
When to use this technique

Use lace pressing on fresh, soft clay before the surface begins to stiffen. The clay needs to be pliable enough to deform under pressure but firm enough not to stick aggressively to the fabric. Most air-dry clay and paper clay is at the right stage for the first 20 minutes after rolling. Once the surface starts to lose its sheen, it is too late for a clean impression.
What you need
Air-dry clay rolled to 8-10 mm thick slab
A piece of cotton lace, mesh fabric, or any open-weave material larger than the slab
Rolling pin or smooth dowel
Smooth board surface
Step by step
Roll the clay slab to an even 8 mm thickness on the board. Work quickly to use the slab while fresh.
Lay the lace flat over the surface of the slab, smoothing out any folds or creases.
Place the rolling pin at one end of the lace and roll firmly and evenly from one end to the other in a single pass. Do not rock or repeat; a single firm pass gives the cleanest impression.
Peel the lace away from one corner, lifting smoothly and steadily. If the lace sticks, press it lightly back down at the stuck area and pull from the opposite side.
Examine the impression. The pattern should be crisp, 1-2 mm deep. If too shallow, re-roll with more pressure; if the clay tore, the pressure was too high or the clay too dry.
What a good impression looks like

The pattern of the lace should be clearly readable across the full surface. The high points of the lace create recessed channels in the clay; the openings in the lace leave raised ridges. At the edges of the slab, the texture should extend uniformly to the cut edge with no fading or blurring. Fingerprints and tool marks should not appear alongside the texture.
Using the impressed slab

Cut the impressed slab to the required shape with a fettling knife while still fresh. Use as the decorative face of a dish, the front panel of a coil-built box, or as a wall tile. When dry, the texture takes paint and glaze differently from the flat surface, giving two-tone effects without any second colour being applied.
Greenware
lace-impressed clay can also be painted with a thin wash of iron oxide, then wiped back to highlight the raised areas.
Common mistakes
Rolling the lace more than once, which blurs the first impression.
Using lace with very fine threads makes the pattern too shallow to read. Open-weave cotton lace gives the cleanest results.
Pressing onto clay that has begun to dry. The surface tears rather than deforms cleanly.
Moving the lace sideways during peeling, which smears the impression. Lift straight up.
